content:
# Joshua Chapter 5 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 228–229 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_005_page_001.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 165–166 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** And it was, when all the kings of the Amorites who were beyond the Jordan to the west, and all the kings of the Canaanites who were by the sea, heard that YHWH had dried up the waters of the Jordan before the sons of Israel until they had crossed over, that their hearts melted, and there was no longer any spirit in them because of the sons of Israel.

**2** At that time YHWH said to Joshua, "Make for yourself flint knives and circumcise the sons of Israel again, a second time."

**3** So Joshua made for himself flint knives and circumcised the sons of Israel at the Hill of the Foreskins.

**4** And this is the reason that Joshua circumcised them: all the people who came out of Egypt, the males — all the men of war — had died in the wilderness on the way after they came out of Egypt.

**5** For all the people who came out were circumcised, but all the people who were born in the wilderness on the way as they came out of Egypt had not been circumcised.

**6** For the sons of Israel walked forty years in the wilderness, until all the nation — the men of war who came out of Egypt — were finished, those who did not listen to the voice of YHWH, to whom YHWH swore that he would not let them see the land that YHWH had sworn to their fathers to give us, a land flowing with milk and honey.

**7** And their sons he raised up in their place; these Joshua circumcised, for they were uncircumcised, because they had not been circumcised on the way.

**8** And it was, when all the nation had finished being circumcised, that they remained in their places in the camp until they were healed.

**9** And YHWH said to Joshua, "Today I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt from upon you." So the name of that place has been called Gilgal to this day.

**10** And the sons of Israel encamped at Gilgal, and they kept the Passover on the fourteenth day of the month in the evening, in the plains of Jericho.

**11** And they ate from the produce of the land on the day after the Passover — unleavened cakes and parched grain — on this very day.

**12** And the manna ceased on the day after they ate from the produce of the land, and there was no longer manna for the sons of Israel. And they ate from the yield of the land of Canaan in that year.

**13** And it was, when Joshua was by Jericho, that he lifted up his eyes and looked, and behold, a man was standing before him with his drawn sword in his hand. And Joshua went to him and said to him, "Are you for us, or for our adversaries?"

**14** And he said, "No, for I am the commander of the army of YHWH. Now I have come." And Joshua fell on his face to the earth and bowed down, and said to him, "What does my lord speak to his servant?"

**15** And the commander of the army of YHWH said to Joshua, "Remove your sandal from upon your foot, for the place on which you are standing is holy." And Joshua did so.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Joshua 5 spans Pages 228–229.

   **Page 228** (JOS_005_page_001.jpg): This page displays the characteristic three-column layout of the Leningrad Codex. Joshua 5 begins in the left column area following the conclusion of Joshua 4. The chapter opens with the report of the Amorite and Canaanite kings' terror upon hearing of the Jordan crossing (v. 1). The divine command for circumcision with flint knives (v. 2), the execution at Gibeath-haaraloth (v. 3), and the extended narrative explanation for why circumcision was necessary (vv. 4–7) are legible through the columns. The text continues through the healing period at Gilgal (v. 8) and the divine declaration rolling away the reproach of Egypt (v. 9). The Masoretic vowel pointing and cantillation marks are clearly visible throughout.

   **Page 229** (JOS_005_page_002.jpg): This page continues Joshua 5 with the Passover observance at Gilgal (v. 10), the eating of the produce of the land (v. 11), the cessation of the manna (v. 12), and the theophanic encounter with the commander of YHWH's army (vv. 13–15). The chapter concludes on this page, and Joshua 6 begins following a parasha break. The Masorah Parva notations are visible in the margins.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Pages 165–166 (JOS_005_vaticanus_page_001.jpg and JOS_005_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) contain the Joshua 5 material in the Vaticanus's three-column continuous uncial script without verse divisions. Page 165 carries material from the end of Joshua 4 into the opening of Joshua 5, with the narrative of the kings' fear and the circumcision commands. The chapter heading "CAP. ΧΙΧ" is visible at the right-hand margin area. Page 166, headed "CAP. ΧΙΧ" continuing into subsequent sections, carries the remainder of Joshua 5 — the Passover, the cessation of manna, and the theophanic encounter — before transitioning into Joshua 6. The LXX text of Joshua 5 has several significant variants from the MT, detailed below.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. חַרְבוֹת צֻרִים — "Flint Knives" (v. 2) — The Instrument of Circumcision**

YHWH's command in verse 2 specifies the instrument for circumcision: חַרְבוֹת צֻרִים (charvot tsurim, "knives of flint"). The word חֶרֶב (cherev) normally means "sword" but here, in the construct plural חַרְבוֹת (charvot), refers to cutting implements — "knives." The material specified is צֹר (tsor) or צוּר (tsur, "flint, sharp stone"). The use of flint for circumcision is attested elsewhere in the Pentateuch: Zipporah uses a צֹר (tsor, "flint") to circumcise her son in Exodus 4:25.

The specification of flint rather than metal is significant. By the time of the conquest, Israel certainly had access to bronze implements, and the Iron Age was dawning. The insistence on flint for circumcision appears to reflect a deliberate archaism — the ritual instrument preserves an older technology, investing the rite with the weight of antiquity. This pattern of ritual conservatism (using archaic materials for sacred acts) is well attested in the ancient Near East.

The formal translation renders "flint knives" — reversing the Hebrew construct order (knives-of-flint) into natural English word order (flint knives), which is the standard handling of Hebrew construct chains. The LXX renders μαχαίρας πετρίνας (machairas petrinas, "stone knives"), using πέτρινος (petrinos, "made of stone") — a broader term than "flint" specifically, but conveying the same archaic material.

**2. שׁוּב — "Again, a Second Time" (v. 2) — The Meaning of שׁוּב מוּל**

The command שׁוּב מֹל (shuv mol, "return [to] circumcise" or "circumcise again") has generated interpretive discussion. The verb שׁוּב (shuv, "to return, to do again") combined with the imperative מֹל (mol, "circumcise") and the adverbial clarification שֵׁנִית (shenit, "a second time") creates a twofold emphasis on repetition. This does not mean that the same individuals were circumcised twice. The "again" refers to the act of circumcision as a national practice: Israel had circumcised the generation that left Egypt, but during the forty years of wilderness wandering, the new generation born in the wilderness had not been circumcised. Joshua is commanded to resume the practice — to "circumcise again" in the sense of restoring circumcision to the nation as a whole.

The formal translation renders "circumcise the sons of Israel again, a second time" — preserving both שׁוּב ("again") and שֵׁנִית ("a second time") as they appear in the Hebrew, allowing the reader to perceive the emphasis.

**3. גִּבְעַת הָעֲרָלוֹת — "The Hill of the Foreskins" (v. 3) — The Place Name**

The place name גִּבְעַת הָעֲרָלוֹת (Giv'at ha'Aralot) is a construct phrase: גִּבְעָה (giv'ah, "hill") + הָעֲרָלוֹת (ha'aralot, "the foreskins"). The formal translation renders the place name in English as "the Hill of the Foreskins" rather than transliterating it as "Gibeath-haaraloth," because the Hebrew text intends the name to be descriptive and meaningful — the place was named for what happened there. The reader should understand the place name, not merely hear an opaque transliteration.

The LXX renders τὸν βουνὸν τῶν ἀκροβυστιῶν (ton bounon ton akrobystion, "the hill of the foreskins"), translating the name rather than transliterating — confirming that the descriptive meaning was understood in the Greek tradition as well.

**4. חֶרְפַּת מִצְרַיִם — "The Reproach of Egypt" (v. 9) — What Was Rolled Away**

YHWH's declaration in verse 9 — הַיּוֹם גַּלּוֹתִי אֶת חֶרְפַּת מִצְרַיִם מֵעֲלֵיכֶם (hayom galloti et cherpat Mitsrayim me'aleikhem, "Today I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt from upon you") — contains a wordplay that gives Gilgal its name. The verb גָּלַל (galal, "to roll") is the etymological root of the name גִּלְגָּל (Gilgal, "rolling" or "circle"). YHWH "rolled away" (galloti) the reproach, and so the place is called Gilgal ("Rolling").

The "reproach of Egypt" (חֶרְפַּת מִצְרַיִם, cherpat Mitsrayim) is itself ambiguous. The primary interpretations are:

- **The reproach that Egypt cast upon Israel:** The taunting of the Egyptians — their mockery that Israel had been brought out of Egypt only to die in the wilderness (cf. Exodus 32:12; Numbers 14:13–16; Deuteronomy 9:28). The circumcision of the new generation and their entry into the promised land ends this reproach — Egypt can no longer mock Israel's deliverance.

- **The reproach of having been slaves in Egypt:** The stigma of slavery itself — the shame of servitude and its lingering mark upon a people. By entering the land and circumcising the new generation, Israel's identity as a free, covenant people replaces the identity of enslaved people.

- **The uncircumcised state itself as "Egyptian":** Since the wilderness generation had not been circumcised, they bore on their bodies what amounted to an Egyptian condition — the uncircumcision that characterized the nations. The circumcision at Gilgal removes this and restores their covenantal identity.

The formal translation renders "the reproach of Egypt" without specifying which interpretation is intended — the Hebrew genitive construction (cherpat Mitsrayim) is deliberately ambiguous between subjective genitive ("the reproach that Egypt imposed") and objective genitive ("the reproach associated with Egypt"), and the formal translation preserves this ambiguity.

**5. גִּלְגָּל — "Gilgal" (v. 9) — The Etymological Wordplay**

The name גִּלְגָּל (Gilgal) is etymologized through the verb גַּלּוֹתִי (galloti, "I have rolled away"), from the root גָּלַל (galal, "to roll"). The reduplication pattern of Gilgal (gal-gal) is a common Hebrew intensive or iterative formation — it suggests ongoing or complete rolling. The formal translation preserves the place name "Gilgal" and renders the verb "rolled away," allowing the English reader to perceive the phonetic connection between "rolled" (gal-) and "Gilgal" (Gilgal).

The etiological formula "So the name of that place has been called Gilgal to this day" (וַיִּקְרָא שֵׁם הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא גִּלְגָּל עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה) follows the standard Hebrew etiological pattern found throughout Joshua (cf. 4:9; 7:26; 8:28–29).

**6. מִמָּחֳרַת הַפֶּסַח — "On the Day After the Passover" (v. 11) — The Calendar Question**

Verse 11 states that the Israelites ate from the produce of the land מִמָּחֳרָת הַפֶּסַח (mimmocharot haPesach, "on the day after the Passover"). If the Passover was observed on the evening of the fourteenth of Nisan (as v. 10 states), then "the day after the Passover" is the fifteenth of Nisan — the first day of the Feast of Unleavened Bread. The food they ate — מַצּוֹת (matsot, "unleavened cakes") and קָלוּי (qalui, "parched/roasted grain") — is precisely the food of the Unleavened Bread festival.

The manna then ceased on the following day (v. 12) — מִמָּחֳרָת (mimmocharot, "on the day after"), which would be the sixteenth of Nisan. This sequence marks a decisive transition: from manna (God's wilderness provision) to the produce of the land (God's Canaan provision). The formal translation preserves the temporal markers exactly as the Hebrew presents them.

**7. וַיִּשְׁבֹּת הַמָּן — "And the Manna Ceased" (v. 12) — The End of Wilderness Provision**

The verb וַיִּשְׁבֹּת (vayyishbot, "and it ceased") is from the root שָׁבַת (shavat, "to cease, to stop, to rest") — the same root that gives us שַׁבָּת (Shabbat, "Sabbath, rest"). The manna "sabbathed" — it rested, it ceased. The wordplay is latent: the manna's cessation is described with the vocabulary of rest and completion. The provision that sustained Israel for forty years comes to its appointed end when the need for it is fulfilled by the produce of the promised land.

The formal translation renders "the manna ceased" — the most direct rendering of vayyishbot. The deeper resonance with Shabbat is noted here but cannot be replicated in English without overtranslation.

**8. שַׂר צְבָא יְהוָה — "The Commander of the Army of YHWH" (vv. 14–15) — The Theophanic Figure**

The figure who appears to Joshua near Jericho identifies himself as שַׂר צְבָא יְהוָה (sar tseva YHWH, "the commander of the army of YHWH"). The word שַׂר (sar, "prince, commander, chief, captain") denotes military authority. צָבָא (tsava, "army, host") is the word used for both human armies and the heavenly host — the angelic armies of God (cf. 1 Kings 22:19; Psalm 148:2; the divine title יְהוָה צְבָאוֹת, YHWH Tsevaot, "YHWH of Hosts/Armies"). The figure is the supreme military commander of YHWH's heavenly forces.

The identity of this figure has been extensively discussed:

- **An angel of high rank:** The commander of God's celestial army, analogous to Michael in Daniel 10:13, 21; 12:1, who is called שַׂר הַגָּדוֹל (sar haggadol, "the great prince"). On this reading, the figure is a created being of supreme angelic authority.

- **The Angel of YHWH (מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה):** Many interpreters identify this figure with the Angel of YHWH who appears throughout the Pentateuch and Former Prophets — the figure who speaks as God, bears the divine name, and accepts worship. The command to remove sandals because the ground is holy (v. 15) directly echoes the burning bush theophany (Exodus 3:5), where it is YHWH himself who speaks from the bush. This parallel strongly suggests that the commander of YHWH's army is a theophanic figure — a manifestation of divine presence in visible form.

- **A pre-incarnate appearance of the divine Son:** Christian theological tradition, building on the Angel-of-YHWH identification and the Exodus 3:5 parallel, has frequently identified this figure as a Christophany — an Old Testament appearance of the second person of the Trinity.

The formal translation renders "the commander of the army of YHWH" — following the Hebrew construct chain precisely: שַׂר (commander) + צְבָא (army) + יְהוָה (YHWH). The title "commander" is preferred over "captain" (KJV) or "prince" (some translations) because שַׂר in a military context denotes the person who commands forces, and "commander" conveys this most naturally in modern English.

**9. לֹא — "No" (v. 14) — The Commander's Response to Joshua's Question**

Joshua's question in verse 13 — הֲלָנוּ אַתָּה אִם לְצָרֵינוּ (halanu attah im letsarenu, "Are you for us, or for our adversaries?") — receives the startling reply: לֹא (lo, "No"). This is not "No, I am for your adversaries" — it is a categorical rejection of the binary Joshua has proposed. The commander of YHWH's army does not come to take sides; he comes as the one to whom both sides must answer. The divine warrior is not Israel's partisan; Israel is to be the divine warrior's servant.

The formal translation renders the single word לֹא as "No" — preserving the abruptness and the refusal to be categorized. The explanatory clause follows: כִּי אֲנִי שַׂר צְבָא יְהוָה עַתָּה בָאתִי (ki ani sar tseva YHWH attah vati, "for I am the commander of the army of YHWH; now I have come"). The כִּי (ki, "for, because") introduces the reason for the "No" — his identity transcends the categories of "for us" or "against us."

**10. שַׁל נַעַלְךָ מֵעַל רַגְלֶךָ — "Remove Your Sandal from upon Your Foot" (v. 15) — The Exodus 3:5 Parallel**

The command to Joshua — שַׁל נַעַלְךָ מֵעַל רַגְלֶךָ כִּי הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה עֹמֵד עָלָיו קֹדֶשׁ הוּא (shal na'alkha me'al raglekha ki hamaqom asher attah omed alav qodesh hu, "Remove your sandal from upon your foot, for the place on which you are standing is holy") — is a near-exact repetition of the divine command to Moses at the burning bush:

- Exodus 3:5: שַׁל נְעָלֶיךָ מֵעַל רַגְלֶיךָ כִּי הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה עוֹמֵד עָלָיו אַדְמַת קֹדֶשׁ הוּא
  ("Remove your sandals from upon your feet, for the place on which you are standing is holy ground.")

The differences are minimal: Moses is told to remove both sandals (plural נְעָלֶיךָ, ne'alekha, "your sandals"; plural רַגְלֶיךָ, raglekha, "your feet"), while Joshua is told to remove one sandal (singular נַעַלְךָ, na'alkha, "your sandal"; singular רַגְלֶךָ, raglekha, "your foot"). Exodus 3:5 adds אַדְמַת (admat, "ground of") before קֹדֶשׁ (qodesh, "holiness") — "holy ground" — while Joshua 5:15 says simply קֹדֶשׁ הוּא (qodesh hu, "it is holy").

The literary function of this parallel is to establish Joshua as the successor of Moses. Moses received the theophanic commission at the burning bush before the Exodus; Joshua receives the theophanic commission near Jericho before the conquest. Both are told to remove their sandals; both stand on holy ground; both receive their commission from a divine figure. The narrative deliberately echoes the Mosaic moment to validate Joshua's authority as the new leader of YHWH's people.

The formal translation renders "Remove your sandal from upon your foot" — preserving the singular number of the MT. Some translations harmonize with Exodus 3:5 and render the plural; the formal translation follows the Leningrad Codex precisely.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Joshua 5

The Vaticanus manuscript pages 165–166 (JOS_005_vaticanus_page_001.jpg and JOS_005_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) were read directly. The LXX text of Joshua diverges from the MT more significantly than in the Pentateuch, and Joshua 5 contains several notable variants:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5:1 | מַלְכֵי הָאֱמֹרִי... מַלְכֵי הַכְּנַעֲנִי ("kings of the Amorites... kings of the Canaanites") | βασιλεῖς τῶν Αμορραίων... βασιλεῖς τῆς Φοινίκης ("kings of the Amorites... kings of Phoenicia") | "kings of the Amorites... kings of the Canaanites" | LXX renders כְּנַעֲנִי as Φοινίκη (Phoinike, "Phoenicia") — reflecting the identification of Canaan with the Phoenician coast, a known LXX tendency |
| 5:2 | חַרְבוֹת צֻרִים ("knives of flint") | μαχαίρας πετρίνας ("stone knives") | "flint knives" | LXX uses πέτρινος ("stone") — a broader term; both indicate non-metal instruments |
| 5:3 | גִּבְעַת הָעֲרָלוֹת ("Hill of the Foreskins") | τὸν βουνὸν τῶν ἀκροβυστιῶν ("hill of the foreskins") | "Hill of the Foreskins" | Exact equivalence; LXX translates the place name rather than transliterating |
| 5:6 | אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבָשׁ ("a land flowing with milk and honey") | γῆν ῥέουσαν γάλα καὶ μέλι ("a land flowing with milk and honey") | "a land flowing with milk and honey" | Exact equivalence |
| 5:9 | גַּלּוֹתִי אֶת חֶרְפַּת מִצְרַיִם ("I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt") | ἀφεῖλον τὸν ὀνειδισμὸν Αἰγύπτου ("I have taken away the reproach of Egypt") | "I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt" | LXX uses ἀφαιρέω ("to take away, remove") — losing the גָּלַל/Gilgal wordplay entirely |
| 5:11 | מַצּוֹת וְקָלוּי ("unleavened cakes and parched grain") | ἄζυμα καὶ νέα ("unleavened bread and new [grain]") | "unleavened cakes and parched grain" | LXX renders קָלוּי ("parched") as νέα ("new") — a different description of the grain |
| 5:12 | וַיִּשְׁבֹּת הַמָּן ("and the manna ceased") | καὶ κατέπαυσεν τὸ μαννα ("and the manna ceased/rested") | "the manna ceased" | LXX uses καταπαύω (katapauo, "to cause to cease, to give rest") — interestingly preserving the Sabbath-rest resonance of שָׁבַת |
| 5:14 | שַׂר צְבָא יְהוָה ("commander of the army of YHWH") | ἀρχιστράτηγος δυνάμεως κυρίου ("chief-commander of the power/army of the Lord") | "commander of the army of YHWH" | LXX uses ἀρχιστράτηγος (archistrategós, "chief commander, generalissimo") — a stronger title than the MT's שַׂר; δύναμις (dynamis, "power, army") for צָבָא |
| 5:14 | לֹא ("No") | οὔ ("No") | "No" | Exact equivalence — both texts preserve the abrupt negative |
| 5:15 | נַעַלְךָ... רַגְלֶךָ (singular: "your sandal... your foot") | τὸ ὑπόδημά σου... τῶν ποδῶν σου (singular sandal, plural feet) | "your sandal... your foot" | LXX has a mixed number — singular sandal but plural feet; MT is consistently singular; formal translation follows MT |

The most significant LXX variant is at verse 9, where the Vaticanus uses ἀφεῖλον (apheilon, "I have taken away") for the MT's גַּלּוֹתִי (galloti, "I have rolled away"). The LXX rendering destroys the etymological wordplay between the verb and the place name Gilgal — one of the few cases where the LXX sacrifices a Hebrew wordplay that it could have preserved with a different Greek verb (e.g., κυλίω, kylio, "to roll"). The Vaticanus's rendering at verse 14 is also noteworthy: ἀρχιστράτηγος (archistrategós, "chief-commander, supreme general") is a stronger and more specific military title than the MT's שַׂר (sar, "commander, prince"), elevating the figure's rank in the Greek rendering.

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Joshua 5 — Three Movements of Covenant Renewal**

Joshua 5 stands as one of the most theologically dense chapters in the book of Joshua. It is structured in three movements, each marking a decisive transition from wilderness identity to land identity:

**Movement I — Circumcision at Gilgal (vv. 1–9): Restoration of Covenant Identity**

The chapter opens with the collapse of Canaanite morale (v. 1) — the kings' hearts "melted" (וַיִּמַּס לְבָבָם, vayyimmas levavam, "their hearts melted"), using the same verb מָסַס (masas, "to melt, to dissolve") that Rahab used in Joshua 2:11. At precisely the moment of maximum enemy weakness, YHWH does not command attack — he commands circumcision. The entire Israelite army renders itself militarily vulnerable (v. 8: they must remain in camp until healed) in obedience to a covenant command. This is the theological logic of the Deuteronomistic narrative: covenant faithfulness precedes military action. Israel does not conquer Canaan by military prowess but by covenant obedience.

The circumcision restores the covenant sign that had lapsed during the forty years of wilderness wandering. The wilderness generation — those who rebelled at Kadesh-barnea and were condemned to die without entering the land (Numbers 14:22–35) — had apparently ceased circumcising their children. The text does not explain why circumcision was discontinued; it simply records the fact (v. 5) and the remedy (vv. 2–3, 7). The result is that the new generation, born in the wilderness but destined for the land, must receive the covenant sign before they can take possession of the covenant promise.

**Movement II — Passover and the Cessation of Manna (vv. 10–12): Transition of Provision**

The second movement records two connected events: the observance of Passover (v. 10) and the cessation of manna (v. 12). The Passover at Gilgal is the third Passover recorded in the Pentateuch and Former Prophets:

1. **The first Passover** — Egypt, Exodus 12: the night of deliverance.
2. **The second Passover** — Sinai, Numbers 9:1–5: one year after the Exodus.
3. **The third Passover** — Gilgal, Joshua 5:10: upon entry into the land.

The progression is significant: Egypt (deliverance), Sinai (law), Canaan (inheritance). Each Passover marks a covenantal milestone. The Gilgal Passover declares that the deliverance begun in Egypt has reached its intended destination.

The cessation of manna (v. 12) is one of the most poignant transitions in the biblical narrative. The manna — first given in Exodus 16, sustained for forty years (Exodus 16:35), described as "the bread from heaven" (Nehemiah 9:15; cf. Psalm 78:24; 105:40) — ceases on the day after Israel eats from the produce of Canaan. The provision does not overlap; it transitions. God does not give both manna and land-produce simultaneously; the old provision ceases precisely when the new provision begins. The verb שָׁבַת (shavat, "ceased, rested") suggests completion rather than interruption — the manna has fulfilled its purpose and now rests.

**Movement III — The Commander of YHWH's Army (vv. 13–15): Theophanic Commission**

The final movement is a theophany that functions as Joshua's personal commissioning for the conquest of Jericho. The parallels with Exodus 3 are deliberate and extensive:

| Element | Moses (Exodus 3) | Joshua (Joshua 5:13–15) |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Near Horeb | Near Jericho |
| Theophanic figure | The Angel of YHWH in the burning bush | A man with a drawn sword |
| Identity revealed | "I am the God of your father" (Ex. 3:6) | "I am the commander of the army of YHWH" (Jos. 5:14) |
| Command | "Remove your sandals from your feet" (Ex. 3:5) | "Remove your sandal from your foot" (Jos. 5:15) |
| Reason | "The place where you stand is holy ground" (Ex. 3:5) | "The place where you stand is holy" (Jos. 5:15) |
| Mission | Deliverance from Egypt | Conquest of Canaan |

The narrative establishes Joshua as Moses' legitimate successor by replicating the Mosaic theophany in compressed form. What took three chapters to unfold at the burning bush (Exodus 3–5) is accomplished in three verses at Jericho (Joshua 5:13–15). Joshua inherits the Mosaic commission; the same divine presence that empowered the Exodus now empowers the conquest.

**The Drawn Sword — וְחַרְבּוֹ שְׁלוּפָה בְּיָדוֹ (v. 13)**

The commander appears with his sword drawn (שְׁלוּפָה, sheluphah, "drawn, unsheathed"). This is not a vision of peace but of war. The drawn sword signifies that the divine warrior has come ready for battle. The same phrase (חֶרֶב שְׁלוּפָה, cherev sheluphah, "drawn sword") appears in Numbers 22:23, 31 (the Angel of YHWH blocking Balaam's path with drawn sword) and 1 Chronicles 21:16 (the Angel of YHWH with drawn sword over Jerusalem). In each case, the drawn sword signals divine military agency — God's own readiness to act in judgment or war. The conquest of Jericho will not be accomplished by Israelite military strategy but by the commander of YHWH's own army.

**"Are You for Us, or for Our Adversaries?" — The Binary That Is Refused**

Joshua's question — הֲלָנוּ אַתָּה אִם לְצָרֵינוּ ("Are you for us, or for our adversaries?") — presumes a binary: the figure must be either an ally or an enemy. The answer לֹא (lo, "No") collapses the binary. The divine warrior does not come to enlist in Israel's cause; Israel must enlist in his. The proper question is not "Whose side is God on?" but "Am I on God's side?" Joshua's immediate prostration and servile question ("What does my lord speak to his servant?") shows that he understands: the relationship is not one of alliance between equals but of sovereignty and service. The commander commands; Joshua obeys.

This theological point is central to the Deuteronomistic theology of holy war: Israel does not use God as a military asset; God uses Israel as a covenant instrument. The conquest is YHWH's war fought through YHWH's people, under the command of YHWH's own commander.

**The Singular Sandal — A Textual Observation**

The MT's singular נַעַלְךָ (na'alkha, "your sandal") and רַגְלֶךָ (raglekha, "your foot") in verse 15, contrasted with the plural in Exodus 3:5, has generated minor textual discussion. Some scholars suggest the singular is a scribal error or abbreviation; others note that certain LXX witnesses also show mixed numbers. The formal translation follows the Leningrad Codex's singular reading without emendation — the MT's reading is coherent as it stands (one may remove a single sandal), and the formal equivalence principle requires following the source text even in details that may reflect scribal variation.
